description: Additional real, cited safety facts to grow the knowledge base.

records.fact: OSHA's Fatal Four in construction are falls, struck-by, caught-in/between, and electrocution; they cause the majority of construction deaths.
Falls are the leading cause of death in construction; use guardrails, safety nets, or personal fall arrest above 6 feet.
Before maintenance, apply lockout/tagout to isolate hazardous energy so equipment cannot start unexpectedly.
Never enter a permit-required confined space without testing the atmosphere and having a trained attendant and rescue plan.
Hand hygiene with soap and water or alcohol-based sanitizer is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of infection.
Cook poultry to an internal temperature of 165 degrees F (74 C) and keep cold foods below 40 F to prevent foodborne illness.
The food danger zone is 40-140 degrees F; perishable food left in it for over two hours should be discarded.
Carbon monoxide is an odorless, colorless gas; install CO alarms and never run engines or generators indoors.
For heat exhaustion, move to a cool place, loosen clothing, sip water, and cool the body; seek help if symptoms worsen to heat stroke.
Heat stroke is a medical emergency: call 911 and rapidly cool the person with cold water immersion or ice while waiting.
Frostbite appears as numb, white or grayish skin; rewarm gradually and do not rub the affected area.
During an earthquake, Drop, Cover, and Hold On under sturdy furniture until the shaking stops.
Assemble an emergency kit with at least three days of water (one gallon per person per day), non-perishable food, and a first-aid kit.
Keep portable generators outdoors, at least 20 feet from windows, doors, and vents, to prevent carbon monoxide poisoning.
Treat every downed power line as energized and deadly; stay at least 35 feet away and call utilities and 911.
If your clothing catches fire, Stop, Drop, and Roll to smother the flames.
Plan and practice two ways out of every room and a family meeting place outside the home.
Test smoke alarms monthly and replace batteries at least yearly; replace alarms every 10 years.
Place infants on their backs to sleep, on a firm flat surface with no soft bedding, to reduce the risk of SIDS.
Children should ride in a rear-facing car seat as long as possible, up to the seat's height/weight limit.
Never leave a child alone in a vehicle; vehicle interiors can reach lethal temperatures within minutes.
Maintain constant, close supervision of children near water; drowning is fast and silent.
Install four-sided isolation fencing at least 4 feet high around home pools to reduce child drowning.
Wear a properly fitted, certified helmet for cycling, skating, and skiing; it should sit level and snug.
Suspect a concussion after any blow to the head; remove the athlete from play and do not return the same day.
For a nosebleed, sit up, lean slightly forward, and pinch the soft part of the nose for 10-15 minutes.
For minor burns, cool with running water for 10-20 minutes; do not apply ice, butter, or ointments to serious burns.
For a suspected poisoning, call the Poison Control hotline immediately and have the product container ready.
Wear seat belts on every trip; air bags are supplemental and do not replace belts.
Children under 13 are safest in the back seat, away from front passenger air bags.
Designate a sober driver or use a ride service; impaired driving causes a large share of traffic deaths.
Adjust mirrors and seat before driving, and never use a handheld phone while the vehicle is moving.
Boaters should file a float plan with someone ashore stating destination and expected return time.
Operating a boat under the influence is illegal and dangerous; sun, wind, and motion intensify impairment on the water.
Check the marine weather forecast before departure and head to shore at the first sign of deteriorating conditions.
For severe allergic reaction, use epinephrine, call EMS, and a second dose may be needed if no improvement in 5-15 minutes.
Recognize the signs of a heart attack: chest discomfort, shortness of breath, and pain in arm, jaw, or back; call 911 early.
Hands-only CPR (call 911, then push hard and fast in the center of the chest) can double or triple survival from cardiac arrest.
Phishing emails create urgency and ask you to click links or share credentials; verify the sender before acting.
Use long, unique passphrases and a password manager; never reuse passwords across important accounts.
Keep software and devices updated; timely patching closes the vulnerabilities attackers exploit most.
Back up important data regularly using the 3-2-1 rule: three copies, two media types, one offsite/offline.
De-escalation starts with active listening, a calm tone, and giving the person space and time.
If someone may be suicidal, ask directly, listen without judgment, stay with them, and connect them to crisis help (988 in the US).
Mandatory reporters must report reasonable suspicion of child abuse or neglect promptly to the proper authority.
When lifting, keep the load close, bend the knees, keep the back straight, and avoid twisting.
Wear the right PPE for the task: eye, hearing, respiratory, and hand protection rated for the specific hazard.
Keep tractor rollover protective structures (ROPS) in place and wear the seat belt to prevent fatal rollovers.
Stay clear of grain bins during unloading; flowing grain can engulf and suffocate a person in seconds.
Read the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) before handling any chemical to understand hazards, PPE, and first aid.
When thunder roars go indoors, and avoid water, plumbing, and corded electronics during a thunderstorm.
In a tornado warning, go to a small interior room on the lowest floor and cover your head and neck.
Evacuate immediately when ordered for hurricanes or wildfires; do not wait, and follow designated routes.
